section.title: The Cost, the Storm, and the Demoniacs
section.sectionRationale: The miracle collection continues, now showing the King's authority over the deepest powers and the cost of following Him. Two would-be followers meet the hard demands of discipleship; a violent storm is stilled by His word; and two demoniacs are freed across the lake. In each scene the question rises: what kind of man is this, before whom sea and demons yield?
section.thesis: Jesus, the homeless Son of Man whose claim outweighs every other loyalty, wields the authority of God over wind, wave, and demonic powers, so that following Him is both supremely costly and utterly safe, since the One who calls is Lord of creation and the spirit world.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the cost of following the Son of Man; the stilling of the storm and the disciples' wonder; and the deliverance of the two demoniacs and the town's rejection.

units.label: The cost of following
units.note: the Son of Man has no place to lay His head

verses.text: When Jesus saw a large crowd around Him, He gave orders to cross to the other side of the sea.
And one of the scribes came to Him and said, “Teacher, I will {{akoloutheo|follow}} You wherever You go.”
Jesus replied, “Foxes have dens and birds of the air have nests, but the {{huiosanthropou|Son of Man}} has no place to lay His head.”
Another of His disciples requested, “Lord, first let me go and bury my father.”
But Jesus told him, “Follow Me, and let the dead bury their own dead.”

units.label: The storm stilled
units.note: even the winds and the sea obey Him

verses.text: When He got into the boat, His disciples followed Him.
Suddenly a violent storm came up on the sea, so that the boat was engulfed by the waves. But Jesus was sleeping.
The disciples went and woke Him, saying, “Lord, save us! We are perishing!”
“You of little faith,” Jesus replied, “why are you so afraid?” Then He got up and {{epitimao|rebuked}} the winds and the sea, and it was perfectly calm.
The men were amazed and asked, “What kind of man is this? Even the winds and the sea obey Him!”

units.label: The demoniacs freed
units.note: Son of God, before the appointed time

verses.text: When Jesus arrived on the other side in the region of the Gadarenes, He was met by two demon-possessed men coming from the tombs. They were so violent that no one could pass that way.
“What do You want with us, {{huiostheou|Son of God}}?” they shouted. “Have You come here to torture us before the appointed time?”
In the distance a large herd of pigs was feeding.
So the demons begged Jesus, “If You drive us out, send us into the herd of pigs.”
“Go!” He told them. So they came out and went into the pigs, and the whole herd rushed down the steep bank into the sea and died in the waters.
Those tending the pigs ran off into the town and reported all this, including the account of the demon-possessed men.
Then the whole town went out to meet Jesus. And when they saw Him, they begged Him to leave their region.

akoloutheo.gloss: To follow, come after as a disciple. The scribe volunteers to follow anywhere; Jesus tests the eagerness by naming the homelessness it involves. To follow Him is to attach one's whole life to a Master who had nowhere to lay His head.

huiosanthropou.gloss: The Son of Man, Jesus' favored self-title, drawn from Daniel 7 where one like a son of man receives everlasting dominion. It holds together His true humanity, His present lowliness, and His coming glory and judgment.

epitimao.gloss: To rebuke, command with authority. Jesus rebukes the winds and sea as He rebukes demons, treating the raging chaos as something under His command. In the Old Testament it is the LORD who rebukes and stills the sea, Psalm 106:9.

huiostheou.gloss: Son of God. Ironically, the demons confess Jesus' divine identity that the townspeople miss. They know who He is and that He is their judge, dreading torment before the appointed day of final judgment.

basic.context: Jesus prepares to cross the Sea of Galilee. Two men speak of following Him, and He confronts them with the cost: the Son of Man has nowhere to lay His head, and following Him must come before every other tie. In the boat a violent storm nearly swamps them while Jesus sleeps; He wakes, rebukes the wind and sea, and stuns the disciples. Across the lake He frees two violent demoniacs, but the frightened town begs Him to leave.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the three scenes together: each answers the question what kind of man is this. The demands of discipleship, the stilling of the storm, and the mastery of demons all reveal a Jesus of divine authority whose claim on His followers is total. Notice that the disciples' fear is rebuked as little faith, and the demons confess what humans miss.
basic.originalLanguages: To follow Jesus is to attach one's whole life to Him, even into homelessness. He calls Himself the Son of Man, Daniel's figure of lowliness now and dominion to come. He rebukes the winds and sea with the same word He uses on demons, the authority the Old Testament ascribes to the LORD. And the demons, terrified, confess Him Son of God and their judge.
basic.typology: Jesus stilling the sea takes up what only God does in the Old Testament, mastering the chaos waters, so the disciples' question, what kind of man is this, has a staggering answer. The Son of Man recalls Daniel 7, the one given everlasting dominion. And His authority over demons signals that the kingdom of God is overthrowing the kingdom of darkness.
basic.christ: Jesus is revealed as the divine Lord: the One whose word the wind and waves obey, whom the demons dread as their appointed judge, and whose call outranks every human loyalty. He is the lowly Son of Man now, homeless and asleep in a boat, yet the sovereign King who will come in glory. To follow Him is costly, but He is completely trustworthy.
basic.god: The God who rebukes the sea and rules the deep is present in Jesus. He is Lord over the forces that terrify us, chaos, death, and the demonic, and none of them can act apart from His permission. He calls His people to a discipleship that puts Him first, and He is worthy of it, being the Lord of creation and the spirit world.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 107 pictures the LORD stilling the storm for terrified sailors. Daniel 7 gives the Son of Man who receives dominion. Job 38 shows God commanding the sea's boundaries. And James 2 says even the demons believe and shudder, as they do here.
basic.oneStory: The King moves through His world revealing who He is. He calls followers to a total, costly allegiance, then shows why He is worth it: the wind and sea obey Him, and the powers of darkness tremble and flee at His word. The lowly Son of Man asleep in the boat is the Lord who rules chaos and hell, and the whole scene presses the reader toward the disciples' awestruck question and its answer: this is the Son of God.

context.literary: These three scenes continue the miracle section of chapters 8 and 9, moving from healings to authority over nature and demons. The two would-be followers frame the sea crossing, so that the theme of discipleship brackets the display of power. The disciples' question, what kind of man is this, is the interpretive hinge of the whole miracle collection.
context.historical: The Sea of Galilee is prone to sudden, violent squalls funneled through the surrounding hills. The Gadarenes lived in largely Gentile territory east of the lake, which explains the herd of pigs, unclean animals to Jews. Tombs were unclean places; two men living among them, uncontrollable and violent, were utterly cut off from society.
context.canonical: Jesus' mastery of the sea draws directly on Old Testament texts where the LORD alone rules and rebukes the waters, Psalm 107:23-30 and Job 38:8-11, making the stilling of the storm an implicit claim to deity. The Son of Man title reaches back to Daniel 7:13-14, and the demons' recognition of the appointed time points to the final judgment the New Testament everywhere anticipates.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal Jesus' divine authority over creation and the demonic and the total claim He makes on His followers, pressing the reader to answer the disciples' question, what kind of man is this, with the confession the demons make: the Son of God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The miracles describe Jesus' unique authority. The exchanges with the would-be followers are prescriptive in principle: discipleship costs everything and must come first, though the specific words to individuals are not rules for every circumstance. The rebuke of little faith teaches trust in Christ amid real danger.

debates.question: What does Jesus mean by let the dead bury their own dead?

views.label: The spiritually dead can tend to the physically dead
views.case: Jesus is not forbidding filial duty but teaching the absolute priority of following Him; those still dead in sin can attend to ordinary affairs, while the call of the kingdom must take first place.

views.label: A vivid demand for undivided allegiance
views.case: Whether or not the father had yet died, the saying dramatizes that no obligation, however sacred, may be allowed to delay obedience to Jesus; His claim outranks even the deepest family ties.

debates.bearing: Both stress that following Christ is supreme; He does not despise family duty but will not be placed second to it, for He is Lord.

debates.question: Why did Jesus permit the demons to destroy the herd, and how do we read the town's response?

views.label: A demonstration of the demons' destructive intent and Jesus' authority
views.case: The drowning of the pigs shows what the demons meant for the men, ruin, and displays Jesus' complete power to command them; the men's deliverance is worth incomparably more than the herd.

views.label: A revelation of misplaced values in the townspeople
views.case: The town, valuing their pigs and their comfort over two restored men, begs Jesus to leave; the scene exposes hearts that prefer property and safety to the presence of the Savior.

debates.bearing: Both show that Jesus values persons above property and that His saving power confronts people with a decision; some, sadly, would rather He depart than have their world disturbed.

study.originalLanguages: To akoloutheo, to follow Jesus, is to bind one's whole life to Him, even into homelessness, as He warns the eager scribe. He names Himself the huios tou anthropou, the Son of Man of Daniel 7, lowly now with nowhere to lay His head yet destined for everlasting dominion. In the boat He does not merely calm but epitimao, rebukes, the winds and sea, the very authority Scripture ascribes to the LORD who stills the deep. And the demons, dreading judgment before the appointed time, confess Him huios theou, Son of God, naming the identity the townspeople will not see.

typology.type: The One who rebukes and stills the sea
typology.fulfillment: In the Old Testament only the LORD masters the chaos waters, Psalm 107:29; Jesus does it by His word, revealing His deity.
Daniel's figure who receives everlasting dominion, Daniel 7:13-14, lowly in His first coming and glorious in His return.
The kingdom of God overthrowing the kingdom of darkness, as the demons flee at His word and await their appointed judgment.

study.christ: The three scenes converge on a single, staggering disclosure of who Jesus is. He is the Son of Man, so lowly that He has nowhere to lay His head, yet His claim on a life outranks family, comfort, and every rival loyalty. He is the Lord of creation, who wakes from sleep to rebuke the storm and leaves the sea like glass, so that His own disciples must ask what kind of man commands the wind and waves. And He is the Son of God whom the demons confess and dread, knowing He is their appointed judge. The lowly Jesus asleep in the boat is the sovereign King before whom nature and hell alike must yield.
study.god: Here the God of Israel, Lord of the sea and Judge of the powers of darkness, stands revealed in Jesus. The chaos that terrifies sailors, the death that haunts the tombs, and the demons that torment the helpless are all under His command; not a wave rises nor a demon moves outside His authority. This is deeply comforting: the forces we most fear answer to our Savior. Yet it is also confronting, for the same power that frees the demoniacs disturbs a comfortable town, and people must decide whether to welcome the Lord who unsettles their world or, tragically, to beg Him to leave.

crossReferences.note: The LORD stills the storm and calms the waves for terrified sailors, the act Jesus performs.
One like a son of man given everlasting dominion, the background of Jesus' self-title.
God sets the boundaries of the sea, the sovereignty Jesus exercises over the waters.
Even the demons believe and shudder, as the demons here confess and dread Jesus.

study.oneStory: The King crosses His world and reveals His identity at every turn. He summons followers to an allegiance that costs everything, then shows why He is worthy: the storm falls silent at His word, and the powers of hell tremble and obey. The Son of Man who sleeps in a fishing boat is the Lord who rules the raging deep and the appointed Judge the demons fear. The scene drives every reader to the disciples' question and its only sufficient answer, and it presses the same decision the townspeople faced: to welcome this Lord or to send Him away.

quiz.q: What did Jesus tell the scribe who offered to follow Him anywhere?

options.text: That the Son of Man has no place to lay His head, naming the cost of following.
options.note: Verse 20 confronts the eager scribe with the homelessness of discipleship.

options.text: That he must first sell all his possessions.
options.note: Jesus stresses homelessness and cost here; the command to sell all is given to another man elsewhere.

options.text: That he was already too sinful to follow.
options.note: Jesus does not reject him as too sinful; He names the cost of following.

options.text: That following Him would bring immediate wealth and comfort.
options.note: The opposite; He warns of having nowhere to lay one's head.

quiz.q: How did Jesus respond to the storm on the sea?

options.text: He rebuked the winds and the sea, and it became perfectly calm.
options.note: Verse 26 shows Jesus commanding the storm into stillness by His word.

options.text: He steered the boat safely to shore through the waves.
options.note: He did not merely navigate; He rebuked the elements and they instantly obeyed.

options.text: He prayed and asked God to calm the storm.
options.note: He did not petition; He Himself rebuked the wind and sea with authority.

options.text: He let the storm run its course while reassuring the disciples.
options.note: He actively stilled the storm; He did not merely wait it out.

quiz.q: What question did the disciples ask after the storm was calmed?

options.text: What kind of man is this, that even the winds and the sea obey Him?
options.note: Verse 27 records their awe at His authority over nature.

options.text: Why did He wait so long to help them?
options.note: They marveled at who He must be, not primarily at His timing.

options.text: Whether they should find a safer boat.
options.note: Their response was wonder at Jesus' identity, not concern about the boat.

options.text: How He had learned to predict the weather.
options.note: They marveled that the elements obeyed Him, not at any weather knowledge.

quiz.q: What did the demons recognize about Jesus?

options.text: That He is the Son of God and their judge, come at last to torment them at the appointed time.
options.note: Verse 29 has the demons confessing His identity and dreading judgment.

options.text: That He was a powerful exorcist they could resist.
options.note: They knew He was far more, the Son of God, and they could not resist Him at all.

options.text: That He was merely a prophet from Galilee.
options.note: They confess Him Son of God, not merely a prophet.

options.text: That He had no power over them.
options.note: They begged Him, knowing He had complete power to drive them out.

quiz.q: How did the town respond to the deliverance of the demoniacs?

options.text: They were afraid and begged Jesus to leave their region.
options.note: Verse 34 records the town asking Jesus to depart.

options.text: They mourned the loss of their pigs but thanked Jesus.
options.note: They were troubled by the loss, but their actual response was to beg Him to leave, not to thank Him.

options.text: They welcomed Jesus and asked Him to stay and teach.
options.note: The opposite; they asked Him to leave.

options.text: They tried to make Jesus their king.
options.note: Far from crowning Him, they wanted Him gone.

questions: Jesus told the eager scribe about the cost before welcoming his enthusiasm. What has following Jesus cost you, and where are you tempted to want the benefits without the cost?
Jesus said following Him must come before even burying a father. Is there a loyalty, even a good one, that you are allowing to take priority over obeying Christ?
The disciples were rebuked for fear and little faith in the storm. What storm are you facing, and what would it look like to trust that the Lord of wind and wave is in your boat?
The demons recognized Jesus as the Son of God while the townspeople sent Him away. How do you respond when Jesus disturbs your comfort or costs you something you value?
The same Jesus who rules the storm and the demons calls you to follow Him. How does His total authority make His costly call feel not threatening but safe?

translationNotes.note: Son of Man is Jesus' characteristic self-designation, drawn from Daniel 7:13-14, uniting lowliness now with future dominion.
The region is given as the Gadarenes; some manuscripts read Gergesenes or Gerasenes, all naming Gentile territory east of the Sea of Galilee.
Before the appointed time refers to the day of final judgment; the demons know Jesus is their judge and dread their sentence.
